coredata.dc:title: Universal dry synthesis and patterning of high-quality and -purity graphene quantum dots by ion-beam assisted chemical vapor deposition
coredata.dc:creator: Ha, Jun Mok
Lee, Na Eun
Yoon, Young Jun
Lee, Seoung Ho
Hwang, Yong Seok
Suk, Jae Kwon
Lee, Chan Young
Kim, Cho Rong
Yeo, Sunmog
coredata.dc:description: A synthesis and patterning process of luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is highly desirable in applications to industrial- and bio-fields. In this paper, a novel strategy to obtain GQDs is proposed and demonstrated by an ion-beam assisted chemical vapor deposition (CVD). Ion-beam irradiation provides catalyst sources for the GQD creation. The luminescent GQDs are generated using a Fe-implanted Si wafer by a two-step annealing process: i) A formation step of nano-sized Fe catalyst particles and ii) a synthesis step of GQDs by a CVD method. Under the 1st annealing step of the Fe-implanted Si wafer, Fe nanoparticles (FeNPs) are created by the diffusion and aggregation of Fe atoms. Due to the high temperature atmosphere in the 2nd annealing step, FeNPs are then completely removed, and thus only high-purity and -quality GQDs are finally produced. In our work, patterned GQDs were fabricated by using a metallic mask during the ion-beam irradiation. Furthermore, various GQD patterns were also demonstrated by combining the conventional semiconductor manufacturing process and our ion-beam assisted CVD technique.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Patterning
Ion-beam irradiation
Dry synthesis
Luminescence

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported through the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea (No. 2018R1D1A1B07050951 ), and KOMAC operation funds of KAERI by MSIT (Ministry of Science and ICT) .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic layout of a synthetic process for the GQDs through our ion-beam assisted CVD method. The method to produce GQDs consists of a two-step annealing process: A formation step of nano-sized Fe catalysts, and a synthesis step of GQDs by a CVD method. Ion-beam irradiation provides catalyst sources for the GQD creation.

(a) FESEM, (b) AFM images, and (c) Height distribution of the fabricated FeNPs on Si substrates after annealing of the FeISs at 800 °C for 20 min in an Ar atmosphere. Average height of the FeNPs is 4.247 ± 0.43 nm. Cross-sectional TEM images of (d–f) the FeIS and (g–h) the annealed FeIS, respectively. The magnified sections are indicated by white-dashed boxes in (d) and (g). Magnified TEM images of the FeIS at the (e) upper and (f) lower parts in (d). The implanted Fe-ions are indicated by white arrows in (e) and (f). (h) The magnified TEM image of the annealed FeIS. The size of the fabricated FeNPs is ∼4.1 nm. Embedded FeNPs in the substrate are indicated by orange arrows in (h).

TEM and AFM images of the GQDs. (a) TEM image of the GQDs and its corresponding FFT pattern (inset). (b) The diameter distribution of the GQDs. The red line is the Gaussian fit curve. The average size of the GQDs and the full width at half-maximum (FWHM) of the Gaussian curve are 3.21 and 0.59 nm, respectively. (c) HRTEM image of the GQD. The size and lattice spacing are ∼3.3 and 0.25 nm, respectively. (d) A typical AFM image of GQDs. The height distribution of the GQDs in (d) and the measured location is indicated by a white-dashed line (inset).

(a) Raman and (b) FTIR spectrum of the GQDs. (c) The C1s XPS spectrum of the GQDs, measured on a Si substrate. The peak consists of one main component arising from CC (284.5 eV) group and four minor components from C–C, C–H (285.5 eV), C–OH (286.6 eV), C–O–C (287.2 eV), and CO (288.6 eV) groups, respectively.

(a) UV–Vis absorptions of the GQDs in ethanol (red line) and only ethanol (black line). (b) Normalized PL spectra of the GQD solutions excited by various wavelengths ranging from 325 to 500 nm with a step of 25 nm. The relationship between the emission (λ Em) and excitation wavelengths (λ Ex) (inset). (c) Time-resolved PL decay spectrum of the GQDs in ethanol. The red line is the curve fitted by a triple-exponential function and the average lifetime (τaverage) is 2.29 ns.

(a) A schematic illustration of the dry patterning process of GQDs through our ion-beam assisted CVD method. (b) FESEM and magnified FESEM images of the pre-patterned Si substrate by ion-beam irradiation with a shadow mask (inset). Fluorescence microscopy images of the patterned GQD array excited by (c) 340–380 nm and (d) 450–490 nm, respectively. The irradiated and un-irradiated regions are indicated by orange and white arrows, respectively.

Pre-patterned SiO2/Si substrate and patterned GQD arrays through the conventional semiconductor manufacturing process combined with our ion-beam assisted CVD technique. (a), (d), and (g) FESEM images of pre-patterned samples with arbitrary shapes through the semiconductor manufacturing process. The pre-patterned SiO2 layers and exposed Si regions are indicated by white and orange arrows, respectively in (a). The magnified section is indicated by a white-dashed box in (g). Inset: a magnified FESEM image of a pre-patterned sample and the width of the narrow part of the sample is 3 μm. Fluorescence microscopy images of the patterned GQD arrays excited by ((b), (e), and (h)) 340–380 nm and ((c), (f), and (i)) 450–490 nm, respectively.

head.ce:title: Universal dry synthesis and patterning of high-quality and -purity graphene quantum dots by ion-beam assisted chemical vapor deposition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Korea Multi-purpose Accelerator Complex (KOMAC), Korea Atomic Energy Research Institute (KAERI), 181 Mirae-ro, Geonchon-eup, Gyeongju, Gyeonbuk, 38180, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nuclear and Quantum Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), 291 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon, 34141, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Nuclear and Quantum Engineering
Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nuclear and Quantum Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), 291 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 34141, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Accelerator and Nuclear Fusion Physical Engineering, University of Science and Technology (UST), 217 Gajeong-ro, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon, 34113, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Accelerator and Nuclear Fusion Physical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Accelerator and Nuclear Fusion Physical Engineering, University of Science and Technology (UST), 217 Gajeong-ro, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 34113, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A synthesis and patterning process of luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is highly desirable in applications to industrial- and bio-fields. In this paper, a novel strategy to obtain GQDs is proposed and demonstrated by an ion-beam assisted chemical vapor deposition (CVD). Ion-beam irradiation provides catalyst sources for the GQD creation. The luminescent GQDs are generated using a Fe-implanted Si wafer by a two-step annealing process: i) A formation step of nano-sized Fe catalyst particles and ii) a synthesis step of GQDs by a CVD method. Under the 1st annealing step of the Fe-implanted Si wafer, Fe nanoparticles (FeNPs) are created by the diffusion and aggregation of Fe atoms. Due to the high temperature atmosphere in the 2nd annealing step, FeNPs are then completely removed, and thus only high-purity and -quality GQDs are finally produced. In our work, patterned GQDs were fabricated by using a metallic mask during the ion-beam irradiation. Furthermore, various GQD patterns were also demonstrated by combining the conventional semiconductor manufacturing process and our ion-beam assisted CVD technique.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nano-sized graphene sheets that have quantum confinement and edge effects. The quantum confinement and edge effects in GQDs produce interesting physical phenomena such as bandgap variation and diverse electrical changes corresponding to the armchair and zigzag edges, which derive unique optical, electrical, spin and photoelectric properties [1–5]. Compared to the traditional semiconductor quantum dots, GQDs have many advantages including stable fluorescence, outstanding biocompatibility, low toxicity, low cost, and high surface area [6–10]. Based on these advantages, many researchers have studied for various applications: optical displays, sensors, bio-imaging, drug delivery, and photo-catalysts [3,5–11]. However, the ways to practical applications have several obstacles such as patterning GQDs.

The synthetic techniques of GQDs to achieve various applications can be categorized into two methods: Top-down methods and bottom-up methods. The top-down methods represent cutting, exfoliation, electrochemical oxidation, chemical ablation, arc-discharge or plasma treatment and ultrasonication [2–5,12–15], while the bottom-up methods include electrochemical carbonization, microwave irradiation synthesis, hydrothermal or solvothermal treatment and thermal decomposition [7–9,16–20]. Although the methods have their own advantages, they typically use strong oxidants and acids, resulting in the limited applications to bio-field and environmental problems [2–4,8–20]. Thus, many researchers are currently fabricating GQDs by using laser ablation methods and thermal treatment methods with organic precursors or organic solvents [21–23]. However, these methods have difficulties in obtaining GQD arrays or patterns. In fact, some applications to the GQD photodetector or sensors have been demonstrated by drop-coating the colloidal GQDs on the pre-patterned electrodes [24,25]. Moreover, a few methods to make GQD patterns have been reported by using the irradiation of femtosecond lasers to poly(vinyl alcohol) films doped with gold nanoparticles [26] and dipping pre-etched templates in fluorescent aqueous solutions [27]. However, the final patterns produced by these strategies are not composed of pure GQDs (or carbon quantum dots (CQDs)), but consist of a composite of polymer or metal particles with the GQDs, which also may hinder the feasible applications of the GQDs. Consequently, it is still difficult to fabricate exquisite pure GQD arrays or patterns for practical application.

In this paper, we present a simple and convenient route to create pure and patterned GQDs by ion-beam assisted chemical vapor deposition (CVD). The luminescent GQDs are generated using a Fe-implanted Si substrate (FeIS) by a two-step annealing process: The 1st annealing process generates Fe nanoparticles (FeNPs) on the surface through the diffusion and aggregation of Fe atoms. In addition, the high temperature 2nd annealing process fabricates uniformly distributed pure GQDs without any other by-products in the selected areas. Based on this result, we can demonstrate various and well-aligned GQD patterns by using a metallic mask or through a semiconductor manufacturing process via our ion-beam assisted CVD method.

Fe+ ions were implanted into a polished Si (100) wafer (Namkang Hi-tech Co., Korea) using a metal ion implanter at the Korea Multi-Purpose Accelerator Complex (KOMAC). The energy and dose of Fe+ ions were 35 keV and 5 × 1014/cm2, respectively. The current density for the ion implantation was maintained at less than 400 nA/cm2, and the Si substrate was cooled down to minimize the self-annealing of the implanted sample. To fabricate the FeNPs, the FeIS was annealed at 800 °C for 20 min under a vacuum of 3 × 10−1 Torr with Ar gas (100 sccm) and then naturally cooled down in the same atmosphere. The heating rate was 20 °C/min. The FeNPs were used as catalysts to synthesize the GQDs. The GQDs were grown on the annealed Si wafer at a higher temperature than the 1st annealing step. The temperature was ramped up to 1000 °C in 1 h with Ar gas (100 sccm) and maintained at that temperature for 20 min with a gas mixture of Ar (100 sccm) and CH4 (50 sccm). The CH4 gas (carbon source) was flown into the furnace only at the highest temperature (1000 °C). The furnace was then turned off, and the sample was also naturally cooled down in an Ar gas atmosphere. Finally, the GQD sample was taken out of the furnace when the temperature of the furnace was below 100 °C. To characterize the properties, the synthesized GQDs on the Si substrate were dispersed in ethanol (99.999%, Sigma Aldrich) by soft sonication for 10 min at room temperature.

The dry pattering process of the GQDs was carried out using a shadow mask. Fe+ ions were irradiated to Si substrates through the metallic mask. Both edges of the mask were attached on the substrate by Kapton tapes and then the mask was removed from the substrate after the irradiation. A 400-mesh copper grid was used as the shadow mask. The hole shape of the mask was square, and the hole width and pitch were 42 and 62 μm, respectively. The irradiation energy and dose of the Fe+ ions were 35 keV and 5 × 1014/cm2, respectively. A square-shaped GQD array was fabricated using the irradiated substrate by the same process of GQD synthesis as mentioned above. To further obtain various complex types of the GQD patterns, we fabricated samples through the conventional semiconductor manufacturing process combined with our ion-beam assisted CVD technique. Photolithography and wet etching processes were used for patterning a polished SiO2 (290 nm thickness) on the Si substrate. A photoresist (PR) layer with a thickness of 1 μm was first formed on the surface by the spin-coating method (5000 rpm, 35 s). The PR layer was baked for 60 s at 90 °C on a hot plate. The baked PR layer was selectively exposed by UV irradiation for 12 s using the mask aligner (Suss Microtec MA6). Here, it is possible to control the shapes and sizes of the patterns because the patterns of the samples were determined by the used photomask. The UV exposed samples were baked at 100 °C for 90 s and then developed by dipping in the developer (AZ-300) for 25 s. After the PR was developed, the exposed regions of SiO2 without PR layers were etched by an etchant (BOE, buffered oxide etch) for 120 s, and then the covered PR layers were also removed by dipping in acetone, methanol, and deionized water sequentially for 5 min. Consequently, a pre-patterned SiO2 layer with various shapes was fabricated on Si substrates, and it acted as a mask during ion-beam irradiation. The irradiation energy and dose of the Fe+ ions were 35 keV and 5 × 1014–5 × 1015/cm2, respectively. After ion-beam irradiation, the used SiO2 layer was removed using the BOE-based wet etching. The subsequent process was the same as the GQD synthesis mentioned above.

The morphology and thickness of the FeNPs were characterized by a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, JSM-7610F, Jeol) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX, X-MaxN, Oxford Instruments) and an atomic force microscopy (AFM, XE70, Park systems). Cross-sectional transmission electron microscopy (TEM) samples were prepared using a focused ion beam (FIB) method and analyzed by a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM, JEM-2100F, Jeol, 200 kV). The energy loss and atom distribution of the Fe + ions irradiated to Si and SiO2/Si substrates were calculated by the Stopping and Range of Ions in Matter (SRIM) code. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) image was obtained by processing using the Digital Micrograph (Gatan) software. The size distribution, lattice spacing, and height of the synthesized GQDs were also characterized by the HRTEM (JEM-2100F, Jeol, 200 kV) with the Gatan software and the AFM (XE70, Park systems), respectively. The crystallinity of the GQDs was measured by a Raman spectrometer (514.5 nm laser, ARAMIS, Horiba Jobin Yvon). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of the sample was obtained using the KBr pellet method by an FTIR spectrometer (CARY 660, Agilent), and the base line of the spectrum was corrected using the software in an Agilent FTIR spectrometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiment was performed using the Al Kα source (K-alpha, Thermo VG Scientific). To evaluate the optical properties of GQDs, the synthesized GQDs were dispersed in ethanol by sonication. The UV–Vis spectra were recorded at room temperature by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 1050, PerkinElmer). The photoluminescence (PL) and PL excitation (PLE) spectra were recorded using a PL spectrophotometer (LabRAM HR-800 UV–Visible–NIR, Horiba Jobin Yvon), and the PL decay time was measured by time-correlated single photon counting with the Fluorolog fluorimeter (Fluorolog3 with TCSPC, HORIBA Scientific). The PL quantum yield (QY) of the GQD sample was obtained by an absolute PL QY spectrometer (C11347, Hamamatsu). Fluorescence images of the GQD patterns were captured using a fluorescence microscope (DM2500, Leica) equipped with a mercury lamp (EBQ 100-04, Leistungselektronik JENA GmbH) as the light source. The excitation wavelength of the fluorescence microscope was controlled using Leica bandpass filters.

The GQDs were prepared by an ion-beam assisted CVD method that combined both the advantages of ion-beam irradiation and CVD techniques, as schematically shown in Fig. 1 . The GQDs were generated using a FeIS by a two-step annealing process: A formation step for nano-sized Fe catalysts, and a synthesis step for GQDs by a CVD method. Fig. 2 clearly demonstrates the results of the 1st annealing process in terms of AFM and TEM data. Fe + ions were implanted into Si substrates by the ion-beam irradiation with an energy of 35 keV and a dose of 5 × 1014 #/cm2. When the FeIS was annealed at 800 °C for 20 min in an Ar atmosphere, small-sized FeNPs newly appeared on the surface of the Si substrate (Fig. 2a and b). From the AFM data of the FeNPs shown in Fig. 2b and c, it can be seen that the heights of the nanoparticles range from 2 to 11 nm, and the average value is 4.247 ± 0.43 nm.

Fig. 2d–f and g-h are the cross-sectional TEM images of the FeIS and the annealed FeIS, respectively. When low energy Fe+ ions were implanted into the Si wafer, they formed atomically small particles in the Si substrate (indicated by white arrows in Fig. 2e and f). The magnified TEM images clearly show that the small particle distribution reaches its maximum at a depth of ∼30 nm, which is in good agreement with the simulated results calculated by the SRIM code (Fig. S1). During the 1st annealing process, FeNP catalysts formed on the surface through diffusion and agglomeration because forming Fe colloidal particles is more energetically favorable than replacing Si [28]. The TEM and magnified images (Fig. 2g and h) obviously display nanoparticles with a small spot-shaped morphology that have a diameter of ∼4.1 nm, which is very close to the size of nanoparticles (4.247 ± 0.43 nm) as characterized using AFM. Most of the nanoparticles are located on the surface of the Si substrate, and a few nanoparticles are embedded inside the wafer (indicated by orange arrows in Fig. 2h). The FFT results (Fig. S2) of the TEM image shown in Fig. 2g also show that the newly created nanoparticles on the annealed Si wafer are the FeNPs. Meanwhile, it is interesting to note that the size distributions of the FeNPs in AFM data are consistent with the TEM image (Fig. 2g and h). The AFM data of Fig. S3 show the size distributions of FeNPs after annealing at 800 °C in Ar for 1 h and 3 h. Interestingly, the size distribution of FeNPs remains almost constant, regardless of the annealing time. In addition, the distribution ranges are narrow, which means that uniformly sized FeNPs were generated by our ion-beam irradiation method. This may be because the embedded FeNPs inside the substrate were stuck. Thus, the particles could not be diffused to the surface, even after prolonged annealing [29].

Again, TEM and AFM data show the pure GQDs resulting from the 2nd annealing process, shown in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3a displays the TEM image of fabricated GQDs, which are indicated by white circles. The GQDs have a uniform particle size with an average diameter of 3.21 ± 0.59 nm (Fig. 3b). The FFT pattern obviously exhibits GQDs having a crystalline structure (inset of Fig. 3a) [30]. Furthermore, the HRTEM image of the GQDs shown in Fig. 3c reveals that they have a highly-ordered crystalline structure and lattice fringes with ∼0.25 nm spacing, which corresponds to the in-plane distance of graphite (Fig. S4) [31,32]. Fig. 3d is the typical AFM image of the GQDs, and the measured location is indicated by the white dashed line representing randomly chosen GQDs. The heights of the GQDs are 1.5, 1.93, 1.5, and 1.28 nm, respectively (inset of Fig. 3d), and the average value is ∼1.55 nm, suggesting that the GQDs consist of a few layers of graphene [33].

Fig. 4 shows the structural characterization of GQDs prepared by our ion-beam assisted CVD method. The Raman spectrum of the GQDs displays three main peaks at 1352, 1598, and 2700 cm−1 (Fig. 4a), respectively, which correspond to the D, G, and 2D peaks of graphene. The presence of the D + G peak at 2918 cm−1 in the Raman spectrum is attributed to the high ratio of graphene edges in the GQDs. The Raman intensity ratio of I2D/IG is ∼0.4, indicating that our GQDs are composed of a few layers of high crystalline graphene [34–36]. The results are consistent with the AFM measurement above. FTIR analysis was performed to investigate the surface functional groups of GQDs (Fig. 4b). Two sharp absorption peaks are observed at 806 and 1633 cm−1, which are attributed to the stretching of aromatic C–H and CC, respectively. The weak absorptions at 1390, 1464 cm−1 and 2854, 2921 cm−1 are the results of the bending and stretching vibrations of C–H, respectively. The small peaks at 1051 and 1266 cm−1 reveal the presence of C–O and the broad peak at 3440 cm−1 is due to O–H. XPS was measured to further confirm the chemical structure of the GQDs. The C1s peak in the XPS spectra of the GQDs (Fig. 4c) indicates that a small amount of C–C, C–H, C–OH, C–O–C, and CO bonds exist within the main sp2 carbon (CC) structure, which is the elementary unit of graphene. The intensity fraction of the CC peak is 77.39% (Table S1), which is higher than those achieved in other researches [8,21,27]. Consequently, the Raman, FTIR, and XPS analysis supports that our GQDs are of high crystallinity and high purity with few functional groups at the surface of GQDs. In addition, Fig. S5 shows the XPS and EDX data of the GQDs, and the measurements were performed more than 20 times for the randomly chosen samples. The XPS and EDX analysis shows that high-purity GQDs are synthesized, and there is no Fe remnant due to the low cohesive energy of the FeNPs [36–38].

The optical properties of the GQDs were evaluated by UV–Vis absorption (Fig. 5 a), PL (Fig. 5b), and time-resolved PL decay spectra (Fig. 5c). The synthesized GQDs were dispersed in ethanol using a soft sonication, and then the UV–Vis spectra of the GQDs and solvent (ethanol) were investigated for the comparison. No strong absorption peak was observed for the ethanol. On the other hand, the spectrum of the GQDs exhibits one prominent peak at 206 nm with a shoulder peak at 230 nm, which resulted from the π→π∗ transition of aromatic CC bonds. Additionally, one weak absorption at 282 nm was obtained, which may be attributed to the n→π∗ transition of CO bonds or other functional groups [33,39], which is also in good agreement with the FTIR results of the samples, as shown in Fig. 4b. Similar phenomena were observed for the PLE spectra of the GQDs (Fig. S6a). The PLE spectra also show one sharp peak at 252 nm with a subsidiary peak at 275 nm and a relatively one small peak at 334 nm. The energy difference between the PLE spectra is well matched with that between the UV–Vis absorptions. Furthermore, the PLE intensity at 334 nm is much lower than that at 252 nm, which clearly supports that the GQDs are made of a crystalline structure with less functional groups [39]. To further explore the optical properties of the GQDs, their PL QY was measured by an absolute QY spectrometer. The obtained QY of GQDs is 12.03%, which is slightly higher than the reported values (4–10%) [2,4,5,33]. Fig. S6b shows the PL spectra of the GQDs solution measured by increasing the excitation wavelength (λ Ex). An obvious emission peak at 389 nm is confirmed when the solution is excited by 325 nm, and the PL peak gradually shifts from 410 to 545 nm with the λ Ex changing from 350 to 500 nm. This tendency is exhibited significantly in the normalized PL emission spectra of the GQD solution (Fig. 5b). From the relationship between λ Ex and the emission wavelength (λ Em) shown in the inset of Fig. 5b, it can be understood that the λ Ex and λ Em have a comparably linear relationship. The PL phenomena are commonly observed in carbon-based quantum dots, and the exact PL mechanism of GQDs remains unclear. Some suggested that it may be the result of free zigzag sites, quantum effect, electron-hole recombination, laser damage, and edge structures of the GQDs [2,5–8,20–24]. Fig. S6c displays the long-term stability of the GQDs dispersed in ethanol. The PL intensity of the GQDs was measured at various times over a period of 6 months. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of the GQDs remains comparably constant up to 2 months, and decreases only slightly even after 6 months. The high-stability of our GQDs may be because the GQDs have a crystalline structure passivated with a few functional groups [39,40]. Meanwhile, the time-resolved PL decay spectrum was collected to further analyse the PL features of the GQDs (Fig. 5c). The sample was excited at 325 nm. The PL decay curve was well-fitted by a triple-exponential function. The decay times (τ1, τ2, and τ3) were found to be 4.43, 15.81, and 0.61, respectively. The average lifetime (τaverage) was 2.29 ns (Table S2), which was reasonable compared to that of other carbon-based quantum dots [17,19,24].

Moreover, this approach provides a simple way to pattern GQDs by irradiating Fe ions on the selected region. To demonstrate this, Fe+ ions were irradiated to Si substrates by using a metallic mask (Fig. 6 a). A 400-mesh copper grid was used for the mask. Only Fe ions that passed through the mask irradiate to the substrate, which were clearly confirmed from the FESEM image, as shown in Fig. 6b, led to a patterned array. The size of a square pattern is 42 μm × 42 μm, and the pitch between the patterns is 62 μm, which corresponds to the shape of the shadow mask. A patterned GQD array was prepared by the two-step annealing process that used the pre-patterned substrate (Fig. 6a). The fluorescent microscope images of the GQD pattern are displayed in Fig. 6c and d, which were excited by 340–380 nm and 450–490 nm, respectively. As seen in the fluorescence images, the luminescent color of the GQD array changed from blue to green by altering the excitation wavelength. This tendency is well matched with the PL phenomena of our GQDs, as shown above in Fig. 5b. Another thing to note here is that, compared with the FESEM image shown in Fig. 6b, the luminescent region in the fluorescence images coincides exactly with the irradiated area (indicated by orange arrows in Fig. 6b, c, and d). Consequently, the GQDs were only generated at the irradiated part of the substrate with an ion beam. This result is further supported by the EDX data of the GQD pattern exhibited in Fig. S7. The EDX mapping image of carbon, which is the basic element of GQDs, matches the shape of the GQD array, as shown earlier.

One of the most practical advantage for our method is to easily combine with the conventional manufacturing process of semiconductors, which allows for the fabrication of more various and complex types of the GQD patterns (Fig. S8). Instead of a metallic shadow mask, a SiO2 layer (thickness: 290 nm) on a Si substrate was pre-patterned through the semiconductor manufacturing process, and then the mask layers (pre-patterned SiO2) were completely removed after ion-beam irradiation. The thickness of the SiO2 mask was calculated and conservatively decided by the SRIM-simulated results shown in Fig. S1. To fabricate the GQD patterns, the same procedures adopted in Fig. 6a were subsequently carried out. The FESEM images in Fig. 7 display pre-patterned samples of arbitrary shapes through the semiconductor manufacturing process, and the fluorescence images exhibit the GQD patterns fabricated through our irradiation strategy by using the pre-patterned substrates, respectively. As expected, clear patterns of GQDs with various shapes were successfully achieved. The FESEM and fluorescence images demonstrate that the GQDs were synthesized only on the irradiated regions while no GQD was created on the areas screened by the SiO2 layer. In other words, the clear fluorescence images mean that the attainable size of the GQDs can also be controlled by the conventional semiconductor manufacturing process. More experimental results from this approach are presented in Supporting Information (Figs. S9 and S10).

We have presented a novel route to luminescent and patterned GQDs by using an ion-beam assisted thermal annealing process. Ion-beam irradiation can provide a catalyst source for the GQD creation at the selective region, leading to a luminescent pattern array composed of only GQDs. Our bottom-up irradiation strategy has several features compared to the conventional solution-based approaches to produce GQDs. First, no perceptible metal catalyst is left on a substrate due to the high-temperature annealing, thus resulting in high-purity and -quality GQDs. This advantage allows for additional time-consuming and complicated processes to remove catalysts that are not necessary for practical applications. Second, this irradiation process makes it possible to generate pure GQDs only in the selective areas irradiated by Fe-ions, which can provide a simpler and more convenient route to fabricate GQD arrays or patterns than the solution-based approaches. Therefore, we believe that the present approach is very useful for diverse applications such as optoelectronics, nanophotonics, and sensing, as well as energy or environment related applications.

Jun Mok Ha: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Na Eun Lee: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Young Jun Yoon: Methodology, Software, Investigation. Seoung Ho Lee: Investigation, Data curation. Yong Seok Hwang: Software, Validation. Jae Kwon Suk: Validation, Resources. Chan Young Lee: Resources. Cho Rong Kim: Resources. Sunmog Yeo: Conceptualization, Validation, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported through the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea (No. 2018R1D1A1B07050951), and KOMAC operation funds of KAERI by MSIT (Ministry of Science and ICT).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2021.09.071.
